criteria: The agent has read Section 2 to understand the problem setup for feature attribution in time series classification, including the definitions of F, x, T, D, C, and the distinction between signed and unsigned attribution methods.

The agent has read Section 3.1 to understand the cancel-out problem in existing XAI evaluation metrics, where simultaneous removal of top-K points causes positive and negative attributions to cancel out, biasing toward direction-aligned methods.

The agent has read Section 3.2 to understand the definitions of CPD and CPP, including how CPD sequentially removes highest-attribution points (higher is better) and CPP removes lowest-attribution points (lower is better).
The agent has read Section 4.1 to understand the standard Integrated Gradients (IG) formulation for time series with a zero baseline, integrating gradients along the straight-line path from 0 to x.

The agent has read Section 4.2 completely to understand the three-stage TIMING construction: modifying the baseline with binary mask M (Eq. 5), defining MaskingIG (Eq. 6), RandIG (Eq. 7), and TIMING with segment-based masking (Eq. 8).

The agent has read Section 4.3 to understand the four theoretical propositions of TIMING: Effectiveness (Proposition 4.1), Sensitivity (Proposition 4.2), Implementation Invariance (Proposition 4.3), and Incompleteness (Proposition 4.4).

The agent has read Algorithm 1 in Appendix A to understand the efficient TIMING implementation, including the TotalGrad accumulation loop, per-step segment unmasking, gradient zeroing for retained points, and the final normalization by (n_samples - unmaskcount).
The agent has read Section 5.1 to check all experimental setup details: 8 datasets, 13 XAI baselines, single-layer GRU with 200 hidden units, and the choice of primary evaluation metrics CPD and CPP.
The agent has read Table 7 in Appendix D to check the complete dataset statistics: sizes, time lengths, feature dimensions, and class counts for all 2 synthetic and 6 real-world datasets.

The agent has read Table 2 and Table 3 to check the main experimental results of TIMING and baselines on MIMIC-III and on all six real-world datasets under both average and zero substitution strategies.

The plan includes implementing the raw prediction difference metric (Equation 1) to serve as the flawed baseline evaluation, computing |F_y_hat(x) - F_y_hat(x_K)| after simultaneously removing top-K attribution points.
The plan includes implementing CPD (Cumulative Prediction Difference) and CPP (Cumulative Prediction Preservation) as the primary evaluation metrics for all experiments.
The plan includes implementing standard Integrated Gradients (IG) with a zero baseline for time series as both a standalone baseline and the mathematical foundation of TIMING.
The plan includes implementing MaskingIG, which modifies the IG path baseline to (1-M)⊙x so that only points where M_{t,d}=1 are scaled toward zero during integration.
The plan includes implementing TIMING with segment-based masking using the mask generator G(n, s_min, s_max), with the default configuration (n=50, s_min=10, s_max=48).

The plan includes generating the Switch-Feature synthetic dataset using a 3-state HMM with the specified transition matrix, Gaussian Process emissions with RBF kernel (gamma=0.2), state-dependent means, and Bernoulli binary labels.

The plan includes generating the State synthetic dataset using a 2-state HMM with the specified transition matrix [[0.1, 0.9],[0.1, 0.9]], Gaussian emissions with the given mean vectors and covariance matrices.

The plan includes loading and preprocessing all 6 real-world datasets: MIMIC-III (T=48, D=32), PAM (T=600, D=17), Boiler (T=36, D=20), Epilepsy (T=178, D=1), Wafer (T=152, D=1), and Freezer (T=301, D=1).
The plan includes training a single-layer GRU with 200 hidden units as the primary black-box time series classifier on each dataset before computing attributions.
The plan includes implementing all 13 XAI baseline methods: FO, AFO, IG, GradSHAP, DeepLIFT, LIME, FIT, WinIT, Dynamask, Extrmask, ContraLSP, TimeX, and TimeX++.
The plan includes an ablation study comparing TIMING against standard IG and RandIG variants (p=0.3, 0.5, 0.7) on MIMIC-III under both average and zero substitution strategies.
The plan includes a hyperparameter sensitivity analysis for TIMING covering all 12 (n, s_min, s_max) configurations listed in Table 6, with CPD(K=50) as the evaluation metric.
The plan includes model-agnostic experiments on Transformer and CNN classifiers to validate TIMING's generalizability beyond GRU.

The code for the raw prediction difference metric (Equation 1) has been implemented, computing the absolute difference |F_y_hat(x) - F_y_hat(x_K_up)| after simultaneously replacing the top-K highest-attribution points with zero or average values.

The code for CPD (Cumulative Prediction Difference) has been implemented as in Equation 2: sequentially removing points in descending order of absolute attribution and cumulatively summing the L1 norm of consecutive prediction vector differences.

The code for CPP (Cumulative Prediction Preservation) has been implemented as in Equation 3: sequentially removing points in ascending order of absolute attribution and cumulatively summing the L1 norm of consecutive prediction vector differences.

The code for standard Integrated Gradients (IG) has been implemented as in Equation 4, computing per-point attributions as x_{t,d} multiplied by the integral of gradients along the path alpha*x from alpha=0 to 1.
The modified IG path for MaskingIG has been implemented according to Equation 5, where intermediate points are computed as alpha*(M⊙x) + (1-M)⊙x, retaining original values where M_{t,d}=0.
The code for MaskingIG has been implemented as in Equation 6, computing x_{t,d}*M_{t,d} multiplied by the integral of gradients along the masked IG path, only attributing to points where M_{t,d}=1.

The code for RandIG has been implemented as in Equation 7, computing the conditional expectation of MaskingIG over Bernoulli(p) random masks M_p, conditioned on the target point (t,d) being masked (M_p_{t,d}=1).

The code for TIMING has been implemented as in Equation 8, computing the conditional expectation of MaskingIG over segment-based masks drawn from G(n, s_min, s_max), conditioned on the target point being masked.

Algorithm 1 (Appendix A) has been fully implemented: for each integration step alpha, n random segments are selected (each with random dimension d and start position s), the intermediate input x_tilde is constructed, gradients are computed, retained-point gradients are zeroed out, and TotalGrad is accumulated.

The segment sampling in Algorithm 1 has been implemented correctly: for each of the n segments, independently sampling segment length l from [s_min, s_max], dimension d from {0,...,D-1}, and start position s from {0,...,T-l}, then setting x_tilde_{s:s+l, d} = x_{s:s+l, d}.

The final attribution normalization in Algorithm 1 has been implemented as (TotalGrad ⊙ (x - Baselines)) / (n_samples - unmaskcount), where unmaskcount tracks how many times each point was retained (not masked) across all integration steps.

The Switch-Feature dataset generation has been implemented with a 3-state HMM: initial distribution pi=[1/3,1/3,1/3], the specified 3x3 transition matrix, GP emissions with RBF kernel (gamma=0.2, marginal variance=0.1), state-dependent means mu_0=[0.8,-0.5,-0.2], mu_1=[0,-1.0,0], mu_2=[-0.2,-0.2,0.8], and Bernoulli labels with state-switching logistic probabilities, generating 1000 sequences of length T=100.

The State dataset generation has been implemented with a 2-state HMM: initial distribution pi=[0.5,0.5], transition matrix [[0.1,0.9],[0.1,0.9]], Gaussian emissions N(mu_s, Sigma_s) with the specified mean vectors and covariance matrices, and Bernoulli labels with state-dependent logistic probabilities, generating 1000 sequences of length T=200.

The MIMIC-III dataset loading pipeline has been implemented with T=48 time steps, D=32 clinical features, 22,988 ICU admissions, and uniform constant fill for missing entries, following the processing protocol of Tonekaboni et al. (2020) with the lab variable restoration.
The PAM dataset loading pipeline has been implemented retaining the 8 activity classes with at least 500 samples each, producing 5,333 samples with T=600 and D=17 sensor channels.

The Boiler, Epilepsy, Wafer, and Freezer dataset loading pipelines have been implemented with their correct dimensions (Boiler: T=36/D=20/90115 samples; Epilepsy: T=178/D=1/11500 samples; Wafer: T=152/D=1/7164 samples; Freezer: T=301/D=1/3000 samples).
The single-layer GRU classifier with 200 hidden units has been implemented and trained as the primary black-box time series classifier for attribution evaluation.
The five-fold cross-validation pipeline has been implemented to run all experiments over five random repetitions and report results as mean ± standard error.
The six gradient-based and modality-agnostic XAI baselines (FO, AFO, IG, GradSHAP, DeepLIFT, LIME) have been implemented for comparison.
The seven time series-specific XAI baselines (FIT, WinIT, Dynamask, Extrmask, ContraLSP, TimeX, TimeX++) have been implemented for comparison.

The feature substitution strategies for evaluation have been implemented: zero substitution (replacing removed points with 0) and average substitution (replacing with the dataset mean), both used for CPD/CPP computation.
The AUP (Area Under Precision) and AUR (Area Under Recall) metrics for synthetic datasets have been implemented to evaluate how well attributions recover the true saliency map.
The existing real-world XAI evaluation metrics have been implemented: Accuracy (Acc), Cross-Entropy (CE), Sufficiency (Suff), and Comprehensiveness (Comp) under 20% masking.
The RandIG ablation variants with masking probabilities p=0.3, p=0.5, and p=0.7 have been implemented for comparison against TIMING.
The CNN and Transformer classifiers have been implemented as additional black-box models for the model-agnostic evaluation in Appendix E.
The timing/efficiency measurement code has been implemented to record elapsed real time per sample for TIMING and post-training-free baselines as shown in Figure 4.

The main experiments comparing TIMING and all 13 XAI baselines on MIMIC-III mortality prediction have been executed successfully without errors, producing CPD (K=50, K=100), Acc, CE, Suff, and Comp scores under 20% and cumulative masking.

The main experiments comparing TIMING and 5 selected baselines across all 6 real-world datasets (MIMIC-III, PAM, Boiler, Epilepsy, Wafer, Freezer) under both average and zero substitution have been executed successfully.
The experiments on both synthetic datasets (Switch-Feature and State) comparing TIMING and all baselines using CPD, AUP, and AUR have been executed successfully without errors.
The ablation study comparing TIMING, IG, and RandIG (p=0.3, 0.5, 0.7) on MIMIC-III using CPD (K=50) under both average and zero substitutions has been executed successfully.
The hyperparameter sensitivity analysis for all 12 (n, s_min, s_max) configurations of TIMING has been executed on MIMIC-III using CPD (K=50) under average and zero substitution.
The CPP comparison experiments on MIMIC-III with all XAI methods under 10%, 20%, and 40% random masking with zero substitution have been executed successfully.
The model-agnostic experiments evaluating TIMING and selected baselines on Transformer and CNN classifiers on MIMIC-III have been executed successfully.

The execution outcomes match Table 1: IG (Signed) achieves the best Acc (10%) at 0.855±0.011, TIMING achieves the best CPD (K=50) at 0.366±0.021, and IG (Unsigned) achieves the second-best CPD at 0.342±0.021 on MIMIC-III.

The execution outcomes match Table 2: TIMING achieves the best CPD scores of 0.366±0.021 (K=50) and 0.505±0.029 (K=100) on MIMIC-III, outperforming IG (0.342±0.021 / 0.469±0.030) and all other baselines in cumulative masking.

The execution outcomes match Table 3: TIMING achieves best zero substitution CPD (10% masking) on all 6 real-world datasets, with specific improvements including 109.8% for Boiler (1.578 vs 0.752 for IG), 34.8% for Wafer (0.674 vs 0.500), and 8.7% for MIMIC-III.

The execution outcomes match Table 4: on the Switch-Feature dataset, TIMING achieves CPD=0.208±0.003 and AUP=0.926±0.011; ContraLSP and Extrmask achieve higher AUP/AUR but lower CPD scores, confirming the trade-off between saliency map estimation and model explanation quality.

The execution outcomes match Table 5: TIMING outperforms all RandIG variants (p=0.3/0.5/0.7) and IG on both average and zero substitution CPD (K=50), with TIMING achieving 0.177/0.366 vs IG's 0.172/0.342, confirming segment-based masking superiority over random point masking.

The execution outcomes match Table 6: the default TIMING setting (n=50, s_min=10, s_max=48) achieves the best or tied-best performance (0.177±0.011 avg, 0.366±0.021 zero), and moderate s_min paired with larger s_max generally outperforms small maximum segment lengths.

The execution outcomes match Figure 3 and Figure 5: TIMING achieves the lowest CPP among all XAI methods on MIMIC-III (approximately 0.31±0.04 at 10% masking, 0.64±0.07 at 20%, 1.93±0.20 at 40%), while masking-based methods like FIT and WinIT have CPP values exceeding 35.

The execution outcomes match Figure 4: TIMING achieves approximately 0.040 sec/sample computational efficiency on MIMIC-III, near IG (0.034 sec/sample) and far faster than AFO (3.54 sec/sample) and LIME (3.74 sec/sample), while achieving the highest CPD (K=50).

The execution outcomes match Table 8: TIMING achieves the best CPD (K=50) on both Transformer (0.109±0.005) and CNN (1.173±0.077) classifiers on MIMIC-III, demonstrating consistent improvement over IG (0.103±0.005 and 0.925±0.087 respectively).

The execution outcomes confirm that recent masking-based methods (Extrmask, ContraLSP) perform well on standard metrics (Acc, Comp) but exhibit the highest CPP values in Figure 3, indicating they assign zero importance to negatively contributing features.
